exercises.title: Escape Sequences
exercises.description: Escape sequences let you embed special characters in strings. Print three lines using \n (newline), \t (tab), and \\ (backslash) in your strings.
exercises.starterCode:
# TODO: Print a string that contains a tab between 'Name:' and 'Alice'
print("Name:\tAlice")

# TODO: Print three lines in ONE print() call using \n
print("Line 1\nLine 2\nLine 3")

# TODO: Print a Windows-style file path using \\
print("C:\\Users\\Alice\\Documents")
exercises.expectedOutput:
Name:	Alice
Line 1
Line 2
Line 3
C:\Users\Alice\Documents
hints: \n inside a string creates a newline when printed.
\\ is how you write a single backslash inside a string.

exercises.title: Triple-Quote Strings
exercises.description: Triple quotes allow multi-line strings. Assign a multi-line string using triple quotes and print it.
exercises.starterCode:
# TODO: Create a multi-line string using triple double-quotes
# The string should have exactly 3 lines:
# Line 1: 'Dear Alice,'
# Line 2: 'Welcome to Python.'
# Line 3: 'Best regards, Tin'
message = """Dear Alice,
Welcome to Python.
Best regards, Tin"""

print(message)
exercises.expectedOutput:
Dear Alice,
Welcome to Python.
Best regards, Tin
hints: Triple quotes start and end with three quote characters: """ ... """
Newlines inside triple-quote strings are preserved literally.

exercises.title: String Immutability
exercises.description: Strings are immutable — you cannot change individual characters. Methods always return NEW strings. Demonstrate this by showing that original is unchanged after calling methods.
exercises.starterCode:
original = "hello world"

# Apply methods and store in new variables
upper_version = original.upper()
title_version = original.title()
replaced_version = original.replace("world", "Python")

# TODO: Print original — it should still be 'hello world'
print(original)

# TODO: Print upper_version
print(upper_version)

# TODO: Print title_version
print(title_version)

# TODO: Print replaced_version
print(replaced_version)
exercises.expectedOutput:
hello world
HELLO WORLD
Hello World
hello Python
hints: Calling .upper() on a string does not change the original — it returns a new string.
You must assign the result to a variable to use the transformed value.

warmup.title: Quick Recall: Single vs Double Quotes
warmup.description: Use double quotes when the string contains a single quote (apostrophe), and vice versa.
warmup.starterCode:
# Predict the output
print("It's a great day!")
print('She said "hello" to everyone.')
print("Both styles work")
warmup.expectedOutput:
It's a great day!
She said "hello" to everyone.
Both styles work
